title: Gravy on Carpet
stainChemistry: Gravy on carpet is a four-part stain: starch (the thickener that forms a tough film), meat protein (small amount, but it sets PERMANENTLY with heat), animal fat (drives into the carpet fiber and locks under heat), and a brown color from pan browning. The order is everything: scrape starch → cold water → enzyme pretreat (for protein) → Dawn (for fat) → 3% peroxide last (for residual brown). The standard home failure is hitting gravy with OxiClean or peroxide BEFORE the protein is cleared — that locks the protein in and leaves a permanent brown shadow. Hot water at any prior step does the same thing.
whyThisWorks: Cold water softens the starch film without setting the protein. An enzyme pretreat dismantles the protein at warm (not hot) temperature. Dawn then handles the fat. Weighted extraction overnight prevents the pad below from wicking brown residue back up after you walk away. The peroxide step is LAST, only for residual brown — never first. The reason: peroxide on still-present protein locks the protein into the fiber.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): gravy combines starch matrix, meat protein, triglyceride fat, and Maillard-browning pigment. Pro chemistry: enzyme pretreat (protease + amylase) at <38°C, NSD for fat emulsification, 3% H₂O₂ at controlled dilution for residual chromophore. Sequence is non-negotiable — oxidizer before enzyme cross-links protein to fiber.
defaultAssumption: Thanksgiving / Sunday-dinner / takeout-spill context. Fresh gravy, caught in first 30 min = reasonable home win. Dried overnight with starch fully set + pad saturation = harder; tends to leave a darker ring. Pet / kid knocked a gravy boat = often bigger spill than tracked, check a 6-inch radius around the visible stain.

spottingProtocol.technique: Spoon or dull edge — don't rub
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scoop solids (meat bits, thickened gravy) into a garbage bag with a spoon or dull edge. Scrape, don't rub — rubbing drives the starch-fat-protein mix deeper into the pile and pad.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cloth, dry towel + weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dampen a white cloth with COLD water. Press on the spot, lift, rotate, press again. Cold loosens starch without setting protein. Weighted dry towel 3–5 min to extract. Never pour water — over-wetting sinks the mess into the pad.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / enzyme pretreat in WARM (not hot — <100°F / 38°C) water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in WARM water (bath temperature, not hot — hot water sets protein). Dampen a cloth, press on the spot, cover with damp towel to prevent drying. 15–20 min dwell. Digests the protein + some starch.
Work Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Emulsifies the fat fraction. Damp-cloth rinse — never pour water.

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidizer AFTER enzyme step — never before
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if a brown shadow remains after enzyme + Dawn. Hidden-area color test first. 3% peroxide on damp cloth, 5 min, blot. Clears residual browning from dark gravy. NEVER before enzyme step — oxidizer before enzyme sets protein.
Stack paper towels on the spot, weigh with a book, leave overnight. Fan in the morning until fully dry. Prevents brown pigment from wicking back up from the pad as the carpet dries.

steps: 1. Scrape solid bulk into garbage bag.
3. Enzyme pretreat in WARM (not hot) water, 15–20 min — BEFORE any oxidizer.
4. Dawn / NSD + soft brush for fat.
5. 3% H₂O₂ only for residual browning shadow (after enzyme step).
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / pet enzyme)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold to warm (<100°F / 38°C) — never hot, never steam
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets the protein fraction permanently
NEVER oxidizer BEFORE enzyme — sets protein permanently
Order matters: scrape → cold → enzyme → Dawn → oxidizer-if-needed

homeSolutions.instruction: Get a spoon and gently scoop any thick gravy or meat bits into a garbage bag. Don't rub it into the carpet. Don't wipe. Scrape. The more bulk you remove before liquid cleaning, the less you have to chase through the fiber. Look at a 6-inch radius around the visible spot — gravy usually spread farther than you think.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Only — Heat Is the Enemy
homeSolutions.instruction: Here's the rule that trips most people: gravy has a small amount of MEAT PROTEIN in it, and protein sets PERMANENTLY with heat. That means no hot water, no steam cleaner, no hair dryer, no blow-drying the spot, and — most importantly — no putting a hot pan or hot plate near it to soak it up. Cold water softens starch without setting protein; hot water locks everything in. Even if the gravy was hot, you use cold now.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Damp Cloth — Blot, Don't Pour
homeSolutions.instruction: Dampen a clean white cloth with cold water. Press firmly on the spot, lift, rotate to a clean section, press again. Keep going until the cloth stops picking up brown. NEVER pour water on the spot — over-wetting sends gravy into the carpet pad where you can't reach it. Weighted dry towel on top for 3–5 minutes to pull moisture back up.

Dissolve a scoop of OxiClean Versatile or Biz (the pretreat versions, which contain enzymes) in WARM water — bath temperature, under 100°F. NOT hot. Dampen a cloth in the solution, press on the spot, cover with another damp towel, wait 15–20 minutes. The enzyme digests the protein + some starch. Don't skip this step to go straight to peroxide — hitting unhydrolyzed protein with peroxide locks it in as a permanent brown shadow.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Toothbrush for the Grease
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn dish soap with cool water into the spot using a soft toothbrush. Gravy has a lot of fat and Dawn is engineered for exactly this. Rinse with a damp cloth — don't pour water.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for a Residual Brown Ghost (Optional)
homeSolutions.instruction: If a brown shadow remains after steps 4 and 5, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on a damp white cloth and press on the spot for 5 minutes. Test on a hidden carpet area first. Only use this AFTER the enzyme step — hitting gravy with peroxide first sets the protein.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, bleach, or alkaline cleaners.

homeSolutions.instruction: Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, leave overnight. Fan the area in the morning until fully dry. This prevents gravy pigment and residual starch from wicking back up from the pad as the carpet dries, which is why some "clean" gravy spots turn back into a light brown ring next day.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Pad Saturation or Old Gravy
homeSolutions.instruction: If the gravy was a large spill that saturated the pad, if it dried overnight with the starch fully set, or if a brown ring keeps reappearing: call a pro. Tell them: "Gravy on carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold + enzyme (warm, not hot) + Dawn + [3% peroxide]. No hot water used. No oxidizer before enzyme. [Residual ring / pad saturation suspected]." That disclosure is useful — it tells them the protein wasn't set by heat mistakes.

steps: 2. NO hot water. NO steam. NO hair dryer.
4. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz) in WARM (not hot) water, 15–20 min — BEFORE any oxidizer.
6. 3% peroxide optional AFTER enzyme, for residual brown.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if pad is saturated, if ring keeps returning after two rounds, or if hot water / steam was accidentally used early (protein may be set).
warnings: NEVER oxidizer before enzyme — sets protein permanently
Order matters — scrape, cold, enzyme, Dawn, oxidizer-last

neverDo: Never hot water or steam on gravy
Never put an oxidizer (OxiClean alone, peroxide) on before enzyme step
Never scrub — drives mess into pad
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin carpet: tolerates full protocol
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Order of operations matters more than product choice

escalation.when: Pad saturation, gravy dried overnight with starch set, ring returns after two home rounds, or hot water / steam was accidentally used before the cold step.
escalation.whatToTell: "Gravy on carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold + enzyme (warm, not hot) + Dawn + [3% peroxide]. No hot water / no steam / no oxidizer before enzyme. [Residual ring / pad saturation / heat accidentally used]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with combination-stain chemistry

contextQuestions: Turkey / beef / chicken gravy, or brown-gravy-from-packet?
Was the gravy hot when it spilled? Did it sit in a hot spot?
Has anyone hit it with OxiClean or peroxide directly without enzyme?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dark gravy from well-browned drippings has more browning compounds — plan on the peroxide residual step. Gravy-from-packet is higher in starch and leaves a tougher film. Oxidizer-before-enzyme is the bad-advice failure mode — sets protein permanently.
